Complete the following Linux kernel function implementation:

```c
static void hide_si_addr_tag_bits(struct ksignal *ksig)
{
```

```c
	switch (siginfo_layout(ksig->sig, ksig->info.si_code)) {
	case SIL_FAULT:
	case SIL_FAULT_TRAPNO:
	case SIL_FAULT_MCEERR:
	case SIL_FAULT_BNDERR:
	case SIL_FAULT_PKUERR:
	case SIL_FAULT_PERF_EVENT:
		ksig->info.si_addr = arch_untagged_si_addr(
			ksig->info.si_addr, ksig->sig, ksig->info.si_code);
		break;
	case SIL_KILL:
	case SIL_TIMER:
	case SIL_POLL:
	case SIL_CHLD:
	case SIL_RT:
	case SIL_SYS:
		break;
	}
}
```

What is the `dec_data` structure in the Linux kernel? Explain its fields and purpose.

The `dec_data` structure is defined in `kernel/power/swap.c` and is part of the Linux kernel's process_management subsystem. It is defined as:

```c
struct dec_data {
	struct task_struct *thr;                  /* thread */
	atomic_t ready;                           /* ready to start flag */
	atomic_t stop;                            /* ready to stop flag */
	int ret;                                  /* return code */
	wait_queue_head_t go;                     /* start decompression */
	wait_queue_head_t done;                   /* decompression done */
	size_t unc_len;                           /* uncompressed length */
	size_t cmp_len;                           /* compressed length */
	unsigned char unc[LZO_UNC_SIZE];          /* uncompressed buffer */
	unsigned char cmp[LZO_CMP_SIZE];          /* compressed buffer */
};
```

Explain the following Linux kernel function in detail. Describe what it does, its parameters, return value, and any important side effects.

Function (from kernel/kallsyms.c):
```c
int sprint_backtrace(char *buffer, unsigned long address)
{
	return __sprint_symbol(buffer, address, -1, 1, 0);
}
```

Hint from comments: sprint_backtrace - Look up a backtrace symbol and return it in a text buffer
 @buffer: buffer to be stored
 @address: address to lookup

 This function is for stack backtrace and does the same thing as
 sprint_symbol() but with modified/decreased @address. If there is a
 tail-call to the function marked "noreturn", gcc optimized out code after
 the call so that the stack-saved return address could point outside of the
 caller. This function ensures that kallsyms will find the original caller
 by

This is the `sprint_backtrace` function from the Linux kernel's process_management subsystem. 

sprint_backtrace - Look up a backtrace symbol and return it in a text buffer
 @buffer: buffer to be stored
 @address: address to lookup

 This function is for stack backtrace and does the same thing as
 sprint_symbol() but with modified/decreased @address. If there is a
 tail-call to the function marked "noreturn", gcc optimized out code after
 the call so that the stack-saved return address could point outside of the
 caller. This function ensures that kallsyms will find the original caller
 by decreasing @address.

 This function returns the number of bytes stored in @buffer.

It returns `int` and takes parameters: `char *buffer, unsigned long address`.

This is the `set_current_blocked` function from the Linux kernel's process_management subsystem. 

set_current_blocked - change current->blocked mask
 @newset: new mask

 It is wrong to change ->blocked directly, this helper should be used
 to ensure the process can't miss a shared signal we are going to block.

It returns `void` and takes parameters: `sigset_t *newset`.

This is the `force_sigsegv` function from the Linux kernel's process_management subsystem. 

When things go south during signal handling, we
 will force a SIGSEGV. And if the signal that caused
 the problem was already a SIGSEGV, we'll want to
 make sure we don't even try to deliver the signal..

It returns `void` and takes parameters: `int sig`.

This is the `sprint_backtrace_build_id` function from the Linux kernel's process_management subsystem. 

sprint_backtrace_build_id - Look up a backtrace symbol and return it in a text buffer
 @buffer: buffer to be stored
 @address: address to lookup

 This function is for stack backtrace and does the same thing as
 sprint_symbol() but with modified/decreased @address. If there is a
 tail-call to the function marked "noreturn", gcc optimized out code after
 the call so that the stack-saved return address could point outside of the
 caller. This function ensures that kallsyms will find the original caller
 by decreasing @address. This function also appends the module build ID to
 the @buffer if @address is within a kernel module.

 This function returns the number of bytes stored in @buffer.

It returns `int` and takes parameters: `char *buffer, unsigned long address`.
